title: The Pokémon a plain chat model can't name, but Claude with a code tool can
summary: In his "Field Guide to Fable" talk, Anthropic's Thariq Shihipar frames a model's latent, spiky "capability overhang" — the real gains of a new model live as much in the harness you build around it as in the weights.
takeaways: A frontier model's real capability is latent and spiky — the harness you give it (tools, structure) unlocks it far more than harder prompting or a bigger context window.
When a new model ships, its old-harness behavior undersells it; re-discovering what tools let it do is where most of the gain is.
The move that led to Claude Code: give the model arms (a shell, ways to act on the environment) so it builds its own context, instead of a giant context window.
sourceName: AI Engineer — Thariq Shihipar (Anthropic), "A Field Guide to Fable"

sources.title: A Field Guide to Fable — Thariq Shihipar, Anthropic (AI Engineer, 2026-07-06)

body:
## What happened
At the AI Engineer conference, Thariq Shihipar — who works on Claude Code at Anthropic — gave ["A Field Guide to Fable"](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=9fubhllmsBU), published [2026-07-06](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=9fubhllmsBU), on how to work with a new class of models. His framing: models are, in his words, "grown, not designed": so ["what contains them is us"](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=9fubhllmsBU) — the harness and the way we prompt are a function of our understanding of the model. He calls the latent, uneven gains **capability overhang**, with a sharp example: ask a plain chat model which Pokémon names end in "aw" and it fails; give Claude a code-execution tool and it [fetches the list and writes a script to filter](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=9fubhllmsBU) — same knowledge, unlocked by the harness.

## Why it matters
When a new frontier model ships, the instinct is to prompt it the way you prompted the last one. But its real capability is latent and spiky — if you don't re-discover what tools and structure let it do, you leave most of the model on the table.

## How it works
1. **Give it arms so it builds its own context.** Rather than pasting a whole codebase into an ever-larger window, give the model a shell and ways to act on the environment, and it [builds and searches its own context](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=9fubhllmsBU) — the insight that led to Claude Code.
2. **Hunt the spiky unlocks.** Gains aren't uniform: code execution, proactivity, and multiplayer each unlock a specific new capability. The work is finding the tool that turns "can't" into "can."

> A new model's gains live as much in the harness you build around it as in its weights.

## The catch
This is Anthropic talking about its own model, so read the enthusiasm accordingly — and it's a conference talk rather than a controlled result. "Capability overhang" also cuts both ways: every arm you hand the model to unlock capability also widens its blast radius, which is exactly why the same tool-use that unlocks Fable is what this week's other stories say you have to govern.

[Harness Engineering](/guide/harness-engineering/)
